title: ""
date: ""
format:
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    highlight-style: github
    fig-cap-location: bottom
  pdf:
    documentclass: scrreprt
    classoption: [12pt, a4paper]
    geometry: "left=2.5cm, right=2.5cm, top=2.5cm, bottom=2.5cm"
    mainfont: "Alegreya"
    sansfont: "Arial"
    header-includes: |
      \usepackage{sectsty}
      \sectionfont{\color{MidnightBlue}}
      \subsectionfont{\color{CadetBlue}}
      \subsubsectionfont{\color{TealBlue}}
      \usepackage{pdfpages}
    linestretch: 1.2
    number-sections: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    lof: true
    lot: true
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: "NavyBlue"
    citecolor: "NavyBlue"
    urlcolor: "NavyBlue"
    keep-tex: false
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage[utf8]{inputenc}
        \pagenumbering{roman}
        \usepackage{titling}
        \usepackage{float}
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        %\usepackage{draftwatermark}
        \usepackage{longtable}
        \usepackage{array}
        \usepackage{multirow}
        \usepackage{caption}
        \usepackage{imakeidx}
        \makeindex[columns=2, title=Index, intoc=true]
        \usepackage{fancyhdr}
        \pagestyle{fancy}
        \fancyhf{}
        \fancyhead[L]{\small Coral Reef Assessment}
        \fancyhead[R]{\small \leftmark}
        \fancyfoot[C]{\thepage}
        %\SetWatermarkText{DRAFT}
        %\SetWatermarkScale{1}
        %\SetWatermarkLightness{0.95}
        \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0.4pt}
        \usepackage{setspace}
        \onehalfspacing
        \AtBeginDocument{%
          \ifdefined\CSLReferences
            \renewenvironment{CSLReferences}[2]%
              {\begin{description}}%
              {\end{description}}%
          \fi}
    include-before-body:
      text: |
        \includepdf[pages=-]{coverpage1.pdf}

    include-after-body:
      text: |
        \printindex

bibliography: references.bib
# csl: apa.csl
link-citations: true

execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false

knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    fig.align: "center"
    out.width: "90%"
    fig.pos: "H"
---






# Executive Summary {.unnumbered}

This report presents the findings of a baseline ecological survey conducted in March 2026 across seven coral reef sites in Unguja North and Mtwara, Tanzania. The assessment utilized four key metrics—benthic substrate composition, fish density, coral recruit density, and indicator invertebrate assemblages—to evaluate reef health and prioritize restoration efforts under the *Bahari Yetu* project.

Key findings indicate that **Chamba Cha Kati** (Mtwara) remains the healthiest site, with the highest hard coral cover (80.8%) and superior fish densities. Conversely, **Chama Kangu** and **Mwamba Mrefu** (Unguja North) were identified as critically degraded, characterized by high algal dominance (>75%) and low coral cover (<10%), signaling an urgent need for active restoration. The study confirms that Marine Managed Areas (MMAs) generally support higher coral-to-algae ratios and fish biomass, though high-quality reefs like **Nusura** and **Viulu** currently lack formal protection.

Restoration priorities are categorized into immediate, moderate, and maintenance tiers. Recommendations include active macroalgae removal and coral gardening for degraded sites, the establishment of new MMAs for healthy but unprotected reefs, and the implementation of biological controls (e.g., Crown-of-Thorns starfish removal) to ensure the long-term sustainability of Tanzania's marine assets.

\index{Chamba Cha Kati}
\index{Chama Kangu}
\index{Mwamba Mrefu}
\index{Nusura}
\index{Viulu}
\index{Mtwara}
\index{Unguja North}


# Acknowledgments {.unnumbered}

The TNC wish to express gratitude to the following individuals and organizations for their invaluable contributions to this assessment:

- **Mtwara Seascape Team:** Special thanks to the Mnazi Bay-Ruvuma Estuary Marine Park (MBREMP) leadership and technical staff, including Dr. Nedfredy Ngowo (In-charge), Davis Urio, Mussa Hamis, Amos Singo, and Mustapha Issa for their logistical support and ecological expertise. We also acknowledge the technical contributions of Masumbuko Semba (TNC), Pagu Julius (WCS), and The Nature Conservancy (TNC) team members Emmanuel Mpina, Edward Senkondo, and Glory Chagama.

- **North Unguja Seascape Team:** We are grateful for the expertise provided by Yassir Ali Haji (DMC Zanzibar), Hija Khamis Uled (And Beyond Mnemba), and Edward Paul (IMS). Significant technical support was also provided by Catherine Nduati (KEMFRI) and TNC staff members Joan Kawaka and John Komakoma.

- **Local Government & Authorities:** We thank Habiba (Fisheries Officer, Mtwara DC) and the District Administrative Secretary (DAS) office for their guidance on the strategic alignment of marine assets with the blue economy.

- **Community Partners:** Our sincere appreciation goes to the residents of Msangamkuu, Rivula, Msimbati, Mngoji, Mnazi, Mgao, and Nalingu. Their local knowledge and dedication to the *Bahari Yetu* project are fundamental to the success of these conservation initiatives. 


\index{TNC}
\index{NM-AIST}
\index{TAFIRI}
\index{KEMFRI}
\index{MBREMP}
\index{WCS}


# Glossary of Terms {.unnumbered}



| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Acanthaster planci** | Crown-of-Thorns Starfish (COTs) --- a corallivorous sea star known for population outbreaks that can devastate coral cover |
| **Biomass** | The total mass of organisms (typically fish) in a given area, used as a proxy for ecosystem productivity |
| **CCA** | Crustose Coralline Algae --- calcified red algae that facilitate coral larval settlement |
| **Coral Gardening** | A two-step restoration method involving a nursery phase followed by transplantation to degraded reefs |
| **GCRMN** | Global Coral Reef Monitoring Network --- the international standard for reef assessment protocols |
| **MMA** | Marine Managed Area --- a broadly defined zone with regulated marine resource use |
| **MPA** | Marine Protected Area --- a zone with regulated or prohibited extractive use |
| **PIT** | Point-Intercept Transect --- a standardised benthic survey methodology |
| **UVC** | Underwater Visual Census --- a standardised fish survey methodology |
| **WIO** | Western Indian Ocean --- the biogeographic region encompassing Tanzania's marine environment |
| **Phase shift** | Ecological transition from a coral-dominated to an algae-dominated reef state |
| **Recruit** | Juvenile coral colony with a maximum diameter of 5 cm or less |
| **Trophic group** | Functional feeding category used to classify fish assemblages |
| **Allometric** | Relating to the non-proportional growth relationship used in biomass estimation |

\index{Biomass}
\index{Allometric}

{{< pagebreak >}}
\pagenumbering{arabic}

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

library(tidyverse)
library(leaflet)
library(sf)
library(patchwork)
require(tidyplots)
require(scales)
library(ggspatial)
library(ggrepel)
require(gt)
require(flextable)


# Colour palette (consistent across all figures)
site_colours <- c(
  "Fukuchani"   = "#1B7837",
  "Uroa"        = "#5AAE61",
  "Jongowe"     = "#ACD39E",
  "Kendwa"      = "#A6D96A",
  "Mnazi Bay"   = "#762A83",
  "Msangamkuu"  = "#AF7DB8",
  "Mgano"       = "#C2A5CF"
)
region_colours <- c("Unguja North" = "#1B7837", "Mtwara" = "#762A83")

```




```{r}
be_ung = readxl::read_excel('data/TNC Data_Unguja/Unguja coral survey_March 17th -20th 2026.xlsx', sheet = 'Benthic substrate ') |> 
  janitor::clean_names()
```


```{r}
be_ung = be_ung |>
  mutate(
    major_category = case_when(
      substrate_category == 'Live hard coral' ~ 'Hard Coral',
      substrate_category == 'Soft coral' ~ 'Soft Coral',
      substrate_category == 'Turf algae' ~ 'Algae',
      substrate_category == 'Macroalgae' ~ 'Algae',
      substrate_category == 'Coraline algae' ~ 'Algae',
      substrate_category == 'Seagrass' ~ 'Seagrass',
      substrate_category %in% c('Rubble', 'Sand', 'Dead coral', 'Sponge', 'Bare rock') ~ 'Rubble/Others'
    ), .after = substrate_category
  ) |> 
  mutate(site = str_replace_all(site, 'Uroa lagoon', 'Chama Kangu'))


```




```{r}
mtwara_sites = read_csv('data/TNC Data_Mtwara/locations.csv') |> 
    # select(sites, lat, lon) |> 
    mutate(
        lat = parse_number(lat) + (parse_number(str_extract(lat, "(?<=°)\\d+\\.\\d+")) / 60),
        lon = parse_number(lon) + (parse_number(str_extract(lon, "(?<=°)\\d+\\.\\d+")) / 60),
        lat = lat*-1
    ) |>
    mutate(
        lat = if_else(sites == "CHAMBA CHA KATI", -10.313727, lat),
        lon = if_else(sites == "CHAMBA CHA KATI", 40.374487, lon)
    ) |>
    arrange(governance)

mtwara_sites_sf = mtwara_sites |> 
    st_as_sf(coords = c("lon", "lat"), crs = 4326, remove = FALSE)


```


```{r}
#| eval: false

leaflet( ) |> 
    addTiles(group = "OSM (Default)") |>
    addProviderTiles(providers$Esri.WorldImagery, group = "Satellite") |>
    addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron, group = "Street Map") |>
    addLayersControl(
        baseGroups = c("OSM (Default)", "Satellite", "Street Map"), 
        overlayGroups = "Surveyed Sites",
        options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE)) |> 
    addCircleMarkers(
        data = mtwara_sites_sf,
        group = "Surveyed Sites",
        radius = 8,
        color = "#ffffff",
        weight = 2,
        fillColor = ~if_else(governance == "Inside", "#1B7837", "#762A83"),
        fillOpacity = 0.8,
        label = ~sites,
        labelOptions = labelOptions(
            style = list("font-weight" = "bold", padding = "3px 8px"),
            textsize = "13px",
            direction = "auto"
        ),
        popup = ~paste0(
            "<div style='padding:10px; font-family: Arial;'>",
            "<h2 style='margin:0; color:#2c3e50;'>", sites, "</h2>",
            "<hr style='margin:5px 0;'>",
            "<b>MMA:</b> ", governance, "<br>",
            "<b>Lat:</b> ", round(lat, 5), "<br>",
            "<b>Lon:</b> ", round(lon, 5),
            "</div>"
        )
    )
```


```{r}
be_mt = readxl::read_excel('data/TNC Data_Mtwara/master.xlsx', sheet = 'benthic_substrate')

be_mt = be_mt |> 
  pivot_longer(cols = 14:22, names_to = 'substrate_category', values_to = 'pct_cover')  |> 
  janitor::clean_names() |> 
  mutate(across(where(is.character), utf8::utf8_encode))

# be_mt |> distinct(substrate_category) |> pull()
```


```{r}
be_mt = be_mt |>
  mutate(
    major_category = case_when(
      substrate_category == 'Hard Coral' ~ 'Hard Coral',
      substrate_category == 'Soft coral' ~ 'Soft Coral',
      substrate_category == 'Fleshy algae' ~ 'Algae',
      substrate_category == 'Algal Turf' ~ 'Algae',
      substrate_category == 'Calcareous' ~ 'Algae',
      substrate_category == 'Coralline' ~ 'Algae',
      substrate_category == 'Seagrass' ~ 'Seagrass',
      substrate_category %in% c('Sand', 'Sponge') ~ 'Rubble/Others'
    ), .after = substrate_category
  )


```


# Introduction {#sec-intro}

\index{coral reef!ecology}
\index{Tanzania!coral reefs}
Coral reefs are among the most productive and biodiverse ecosystems on Earth, supporting approximately one quarter of all marine species and providing critical ecosystem services --- including food security, coastal protection, and tourism revenue --- to hundreds of millions of people globally [@wilkinson2008status; @burke2011reefs]. In the Western Indian Ocean (WIO), coral reefs are particularly important, sustaining the livelihoods of coastal communities that depend heavily on artisanal fisheries and reef-based tourism [@obura2017reef].

Tanzania's coral reefs span the mainland coast and the islands of Zanzibar (Unguja and Pemba), forming part of the broader East African Coral Reef system --- one of the world's most significant reef complexes [@mcclanahan2014management]. Despite their ecological and socioeconomic value, Tanzanian reefs have experienced considerable decline over recent decades, driven by a combination of anthropogenic stressors including destructive fishing practices, coastal eutrophication, sedimentation, and the compounding effects of climate-driven coral bleaching events [@obura2016coral; @mcclanahan2020global].

\index{marine managed areas (MMAs)}

Marine Managed Areas (MMAs) have been established across Tanzania as a principal governance tool for coral reef conservation, encompassing both no-take zones and partially restricted-use zones [@rocliffe2014towards]. However, the ecological effectiveness of these MMAs varies considerably depending on management capacity, community engagement, and enforcement [@cinner2016bright]. Comparative assessment of reef condition within and outside MMA boundaries therefore provides critical insight into management effectiveness and guides targeted restoration planning.

\index{reef restoration}

This report presents findings from a systematic multi-metric reef health assessment conducted at twelve sites across two regions: **Unguja North** (Chama Kangu, Mwamba Mrefu, Mayale, and Chumani) and **Mtwara** (Chamba Cha Kati, Inner Membelwa, Matenga, Kieti, Rasmo, Viulu, Nusura, and Mmaluko). The assessment evaluated four ecological metrics --- benthic substrate composition, fish density, coral recruit density, and invertebrate assemblages --- to: 

i. Characterize reef condition at each site; 
ii. Compare condition between MMA and non-MMA sites; and 
iii. Identify priority areas for active restoration intervention under the *Bahari Yetu* project.


# Methods
## Surveyed Sites and Geographic Coordinates {#sec-mtwara-sites}
### Unguja North

Unguja North encompasses the northern coastline of Unguja Island, Zanzibar, characterised by fringing and patch reef systems with relatively shallow reef flats (@fig-map-unguja-north). This area is subject to substantial fishing pressure from surrounding communities. Of the four sites assessed, Fukuchani and Uroa fall within designated MMA boundaries, while Jongowe and Kendwa are located outside formal management areas (see @tbl-unguja-geo).

![Distribution of survey site in Unguja North with spatial extent of the Marine Conservation Areas and key benthic habitats zonation.](map_unguja_north.png){#fig-map-unguja-north fig-pos="H"}

Table @tbl-unguja-geo summarises the surveyed reef sites in Unguja North, including site and reef identifiers, management status, and geographic coordinates. By indicating whether each site is located inside or outside designated marine management areas, the table provides essential spatial context for benthic substrate, invertebrate, fish biomass, and coral recruit sampling. This framework supports interpretation of the ecological patterns observed across Unguja North.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-unguja-geo
#| tbl-cap: "Geographic coordinates and management status of coral reef survey sites in Unguja North."
#| tbl-pos: H

be_ung |> 
  distinct(longtitude , .keep_all = TRUE) |> 
  mutate( Management = NA, Exposure = NA) |> 
  select(Area = location, Reef = site, Management, Latitude = latitude, Longitude = longtitude) |> 
  flextable::flextable() |> 
  # flextable::set_header_labels(Area = "Location") |> 
  # flextable::add_header_row(values = c("", "", "", "", "Coordinates", ""), top = TRUE) |> 
  # flextable::merge_at(i = 1, j = 4:5, part = "header") |> 
  flextable::colformat_double(j = 4:5, digits = 5) |> 
  # flextable::bold(part = "header") |> 
  # flextable::bg(part = "header", bg = "#004763", i = 2) |> 
  # flextable::color(part = "header", color = "white", i = 2) |> 
  flextable::width(j = 1:5, width = .9, unit = "in") |> 
  flextable::width(j = 2, width = 1.2, unit = "in") |>
  flextable::align(j = 3:5, align = "center", part = "all") |> 
  flextable::padding(padding = 2.2) |> 
  flextable::theme_vanilla() |> 
  flextable::font(fontname = "Alegreya", part = "all") |> 
  flextable::fontsize(size = 11)

```



### Mtwara
The coral reef assessment in Mtwara was conducted across a total of 8 survey sites, strategically selected to represent a range of reef conditions and management regimes within the region. These sites include Chamba Cha Kati, Inner Membelwa, Matenga, Kieti, Rasmo, Viulu, Nusura, and Mmaluko (@fig-map-mbremp). Each site was categorized based on its location relative to Marine Management Areas (MMAs), with some sites located inside MMAs and others outside. This selection allows for comparative analysis of ecological conditions under different management contexts, providing insights into the effectiveness of conservation efforts and the status of coral reef ecosystems in Mtwara.


![Distribution of survey site in Mtwara with spatial extent of the Mnazi Bay-Ruvuma Estuary Marine Park (MBREMP) showing the key benthic habitats zonation and proximity to the Ruvuma River mouth.](map_mbremp.png){#fig-map-mbremp fig-pos="H"}

@tbl-mtwara-geo provides a clear baseline overview of the coral reef assessment sites in Mtwara, listing site names, reef names, management status, and geographic coordinates. The table orients readers to the spatial distribution of locations sampled for benthic substrate, invertebrates, fish biomass, and recruits, and clarifies whether each site is inside or outside established marine management areas. This contextual information supports interpretation of the subsequent ecological analyses across the study region.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-mtwara-geo
#| tbl-cap: "Geographic coordinates and management status of coral reef survey sites in Mtwara region."
#| tbl-pos: H

be_mt |> 
  distinct(longitude, .keep_all = TRUE) |> 
  select(Area = site, Reef = location, Management = management,  Latitude = latitude, Longitude = longitude) |> 
  arrange(Area) |> 
  flextable::flextable() |> 
  flextable::merge_v(j = 3)|> 
  flextable::colformat_double(j = 4:5, digits = 5) |> 
  flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all")|> 
  flextable::add_header_row(values = c("", "", "",  "Coordinates", ""), top = TRUE) |> 
    flextable::merge_at(i = 1, j = 1, part = "header") |> 
#   flextable::bold(part = "header") |> 
#   flextable::bg(part = "header", bg = "#004763", i = 2) |> 
#   flextable::color(part = "header", color = "white", i = 2) |> 
  flextable::width(j = c(2), width = 1.5, unit = "in") |>
  flextable::width(j = c(1,3:5), width = 1.1, unit = "in") |> 
  flextable::fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") |>
  flextable::font(fontname = "Alegreya", part = "all")  |> 
  flextable::padding(padding = 2.2) |> 
  flextable::theme_vanilla()

```


## Survey Design {#sec-surveydesign}

\index{GPS coordinates}

Ecological surveys were conducted between 16--24 March, 2026 following standardised reef health monitoring protocols [@hodgson2006reef; @wilson2020reef]. Each site was assessed using replicated belt transects at two depth strata (shallow: 3--5 m; deep: 8--12 m), with three replicate transects per stratum per site (n = 6 transects per site). Site locations were recorded using GPS and mapped for repeatability in future monitoring rounds.

## Data Processing

### Benthic Substrate Assessment {#sec-benthicmethod}

\index{benthic substrate!point-intercept transect}

Benthic cover was estimated using the Point-Intercept Transect (PIT) method [@english1997survey], where substrate categories were recorded at 0.5 m intervals along each 25 m transect tape, resulting in 50 data points per transect. These observations were classified according to the GCRMN scheme into seven primary categories: hard coral, soft coral, crustose coralline algae (CCA), algae (comprising both turf and macroalgae), rubble, sand, and other biota or substrate (e.g., rock, sponge). Finally, benthic cover estimates were derived by calculating the percentage of total points occupied by each category relative to the total number of points sampled per transect.

$$ 
C_i = \left( \frac{n_i}{N} \right) \times 100
$${#eq-benthic-cover}

where $C_i$ is the percentage cover of substrate category $i$, $n_i$ is the number of points where category $i$ was recorded, and $N$ is the total number of points sampled per transect. @eq-benthic-cover was used to calculate the relative abundance of benthic components.

### Fish Biomass Assessment {#sec-fishmethod}

\index{fish biomass!belt transect}

Fish biomass and density were estimated using underwater visual census (UVC) belt transects (50 m x 5 m). All fish species observed within the belt were identified to family level, counted, and their total length estimated to the nearest centimetre. Fish density was calculated as the number of individuals per unit area (individuals per 100 m^2^). @eq-density was used to calculate the density of fish in an area.
$$ 
D = \frac{N}{A} \times 100
$${#eq-density}

where $D$ is density, $N$ is the count of individuals, and $A$ is the sampled area (250 m^2^). @eq-density describes the standard calculation for fish density.

Biomass (kg/ha) was calculated from length-weight relationships derived from FishBase [@froese2023fishbase], applying species-specific allometric parameters. @eq-biomass was used to calculate the biomass of fish per area.


$$ 
W = a \cdot L^{b}
$${#eq-biomass}

where $W$ is weight (g), $L$ is total length (cm), and $a$ and $b$ are species-specific constants. @eq-biomass is used to estimate individual fish weight for biomass calculations. Fish assemblages were characterised by trophic group (herbivores, piscivores, invertivores, and omnivores).

```{r}
fish_mt = readxl::read_excel('data/TNC Data_Mtwara/master.xlsx', sheet = 'fish') |> 
  janitor::clean_names()

fish_density = fish_mt |> 
  select(site, reef = location,family:x80) |> 
  mutate(area_m2 = 50*5, .before = family) |> 
  rowwise() |> 
  mutate(total_count = sum(c_across(starts_with("x")), na.rm = TRUE)) |> 
  mutate(density_100m2 = (total_count / area_m2) * 100)
```

### Coral Recruit Assessment {#sec-recruitmethod}

\index{coral recruits!juvenile density}

Coral recruit density was assessed using 1 m^2^ quadrats placed at 1 m intervals along each transect (25 quadrats per transect). All coral recruits less than or equal to 5 cm maximum diameter were counted and identified to genus level where possible [@babcock1992coral]. Recruit density is expressed as recruits 100 m^-2^ using @eq-recruit-density:

$$ 
RD = \frac{\sum n_r}{A} \times 100
$${#eq-recruit-density}

where $RD$ is the recruit density (individuals per 100 m^2^), $n_r$ is the number of recruits counted, and $A$ is the total area sampled (m^2^).


```{r}
recruit_mt = readxl::read_excel('data/TNC Data_Mtwara/master.xlsx', sheet = 'Recruits') |> 
  janitor::clean_names()

recruit_density = recruit_mt |> 
  select(site, reef = location,genus:x7_5_10) |> 
  mutate(area_m2 = 1*1, .before = genus) |> 
  rowwise() |> 
  mutate(total_count = sum(c_across(starts_with("x")), na.rm = TRUE)) |> 
  mutate(density_100m2 = (total_count / area_m2) * 100)
```

### Invertebrate Assessment {#sec-invertmethod}

\index{invertebrates!survey}

Key indicator invertebrates were quantified using visual belt transects measuring 25 m in length and 2 m in width, covering a total area of 50 m^2^ per replicate. The survey focused on ecologically and commercially significant taxa, specifically sea urchins (*Diadema* spp., *Echinometra* spp., and *Tripneustes* spp.) which serve as primary algal grazers; sea cucumbers (*Holothuria* spp., *Thelenota* spp., and *Actinopyga* spp.) which act as essential nutrient cyclers; and giant clams (*Tridacna* spp.) which are indicators of harvesting pressure. Abundance data were standardized to density per unit area to allow for cross-site comparisons. The density for each taxon was calculated using @eq-invert-density:

$$ 
ID = \frac{\sum n_i}{A} \times 100
$${#eq-invert-density}

where $ID$ represents the invertebrate density (individuals per 100 m^2^), $n_i$ is the total count of individuals for a specific taxon, and $A$ is the total area surveyed (m^2^). This standardization ensures that results are comparable with regional Western Indian Ocean (WIO) datasets.


```{r}
invert_mt = readxl::read_excel('data/TNC Data_Mtwara/master.xlsx', sheet = 'Invertebrates') |> 
  mutate(Location = str_replace_all(Location, "Rasmo/ruvula channel", "Rasmo")) 

invert_density = invert_mt |> 
  select(Site, reef = Location, Urchin:COTs) |> 
  pivot_longer(cols = Urchin:COTs, names_to = 'genus', values_to = 'total_count') |> 
   mutate(area_m2 = 1*1, .before = genus) |> 
   mutate(total_count = na_if(total_count, 0)) |> 
  mutate(density_100m2 = (total_count / area_m2) * 100) 

```

## Data Analysis, Visualization and Reporting {#sec-dataanalysis}

All data were analysed in R (v4.5.0; @rcoreteam2024). Site-level metrics are summarised as means +/- standard error (SE). Differences in reef metrics between were assessed using **rstatix** [@rstatix]. Figures were produced using the **ggplot2** [@wickham2016ggplot2] and **tidyplots** [@tidyplots], maps using the **sf** package [@pebesma2018sf] and **QGIS** [@muenchow2017rqgis], while tables were formatted using **kableExtra** [@zhu2024kableextra] and **gt** [@gt]. This report was prepared using **Quarto** within the **Positron IDE**. 



# Results

## Unguja North {#sec-unguja}

\index{Unguja North!results}

### Benthic Substrate Composition {#sec-unguja-substrate}

\index{benthic substrate!Unguja North}


Benthic substrate composition varied considerably among the four Unguja North sites (@tbl-substrate-unguja). Live hard coral cover was highest at **Mayale** (26.2%) and **Chumani** (23.8%), while the sites **Mwamba Mrefu** and **Chama Kangu** recorded significantly lower hard coral cover at 8.8% and 5.0% respectively. Algal dominance was most pronounced at **Chama Kangu**, with macroalgae (41.2%) and turf algae (35.0%) constituting the majority of the benthos, indicating a severe phase shift. **Mwamba Mrefu** also exhibited high macroalgal cover (32.5%), though it supported considerable soft coral communities (20.0%). Notably, **Mayale** was characterised by extensive sand cover (46.2%), while **Chumani** showed a diverse composition including 18.8% soft coral and 11.2% rubble. The major categories are shown in @fig-substrate-unguja.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-substrate-unguja
#| tbl-cap: Percentage cover of benthic substrate across survey locations in Unguja North
#| tbl-pos: H

be_ung |>
  group_by(site, substrate_category) |>
  summarise(n = n()) |>
  mutate(pct = n * 100 / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  select(-n) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = site, values_from = pct) |>
  arrange(desc(Mayale)) |>
  flextable::flextable() |>
  flextable::set_header_labels(substrate_category = "Substrate") |>
  # flextable::add_header_row(values = c("", "Percentage Cover"), colwidths = c(1, 4)) |>
  flextable::colformat_double(digits = 1)  |>
  flextable::theme_vanilla() |>
  flextable::align(i = 1, align = "left", part = "header") |>
  flextable::align(j = 2:5, align = "center", part = "body") |>
  flextable::bg(j = 2:5, bg = scales::col_quantile(palette = "Reds", domain = c(0, 50), na.color = "#c5c1c1ff"), part = "body") |>
  flextable::hline_top(part = "header", border = officer::fp_border(width = 2)) |> 
  flextable::width(j = c(1), width = 1.5, unit = "in") |>
  flextable::width(j = 2:5, width = .8, unit = "in") |> 
  flextable::fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") |>
  flextable::font(fontname = "Alegreya", part = "all")  |> 
  flextable::padding(padding = 2.2)

```


```{r}
#| label: fig-substrate-unguja
#| fig-cap: "Major benthic substrate composition (% cover) at the four Unguja North sites."
#| fig-height: 3.5
#| fig-pos: H


be_ung |>
  group_by(site, major_category) |>
  summarise(n = n()) |>
  mutate(
    pct = n * 100 / sum(n),
    site = fct_inorder(site)
    ) |>
  tidyplot(x = site, y = pct, fill = major_category) |> 
  add_barstack_absolute() |> 
  adjust_colors(new_colors = c(
      "Seagrass"   = "#1B7837",
      "Hard Coral"   = "#dc1021ff",
      "Soft Coral"   = "#7FBF7B",
      "Algae"        = "#D8B365",
      "Rubble/Others" = "#C7C7C7"
    )) |> 
  adjust_font(fontsize = 10) |> 
  adjust_size(width = 3.8, height = 3, unit = 'in') |> 
  adjust_legend_position(position = 'right') |> 
  tidyplots::adjust_y_axis(
    title = 'Percentage Cover',
    breaks = seq(20,100,20)
    ) |> 
  remove_x_axis_title() |> 
  remove_legend_title()

# be_ung |>
#   group_by(site, major_category) |>
#   summarise(n = n()) |>
#   mutate(
#     pct = n * 100 / sum(n),
#     site = fct_inorder(site)
#     ) |>
#   ungroup() |> 
#   ggplot(aes(x = site, y = pct, fill = major_category)) +
#   geom_col(position = "stack", width = 0.7,
#            colour = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
#   scale_fill_manual(
    # values = c(
    #   "Seagrass"   = "#1B7837",
    #   "Hard Coral"   = "#dc1021ff",
    #   "Soft Coral"   = "#7FBF7B",
    #   "Algae"        = "#D8B365",
    #   "Rubble/Others" = "#C7C7C7"
    # )
#   ) +
#   scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0), limits = c(0, 105)) +
#   labs(
#     x       = NULL,
#     y       = "Percentage Cover",
#     fill    = "Substrate \nCategory"
#     # caption = "* = site within a Marine Managed Area (MMA)"
#   ) +
#   theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
#   theme(
#     legend.position    = "right",
#     panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
#   )


```




### Fish Biomass {#sec-unguja-fish}

\index{fish biomass!Unguja North}

Fish assemblages in Unguja North exhibited significant spatial variability in both density and composition (@tbl-fish-density-unguja). **Chama Kangu** recorded the highest overall fish densities, dominated by Haemulidae (90.0 individuals per 100 m^2^) and Caesionidae (28.0 individuals per 100 m^2^). In contrast, **Mwamba Mrefu** showed much lower densities, with Pomacentridae (13.0 individuals per 100 m^2^) being the most prevalent group. **Chumani** was characterized by a high density of Lethrinidae (9.2 individuals per 100 m^2^), while **Mayale** supported a more even distribution of Lutjanidae (9.0 individuals per 100 m^2^) and Nemipteridae (4.0 individuals per 100 m^2^). These patterns suggest that while some sites maintain high abundance of schooling invertivores and planktivores, others like Mwamba Mrefu are significantly more depleted.


```{r}
fish_ung = readxl::read_excel('data/TNC Data_Unguja/Unguja coral survey_March 17th -20th 2026.xlsx', sheet = 'Fish_TIDY') |> 
  janitor::clean_names()

# fish_ung |> glimpse()

## since the fish biomass column does not have the reef, we remap  using the benthic substrate
fish_ung = fish_ung |> 
  left_join(
be_ung |> 
  distinct(location,site) |> 
  rename(site = location, reef = site)
  ) |> 
  relocate(reef, .after = site)
```

```{r}
fish_density_ung = fish_ung |> 
  select(site, reef,family, starts_with("x")) |> 
  mutate(
    area_m2 = 50*5, .before = family,
    x3_10_cm = as.integer(x3_10_cm),
    x11_20_cm = as.integer(x11_20_cm),
    x21_30_cm = as.integer(x21_30_cm),
     x31_40_cm  = as.integer( x31_40_cm ),
    x41_50_cm = as.integer(x41_50_cm),
    x51_cm  = as.integer(x51_cm )
    ) |> 
  rowwise() |> 
  mutate(total_count = sum(c_across(starts_with("x")), na.rm = TRUE)) |> 
  mutate(density_100m2 = (total_count / area_m2) * 100)


```


```{r}
#| label: tbl-fish-density-unguja
#| tbl-cap: "Median fish density (individuals per 100 m^2^) by family across the surveyed reefs in Unguja North."
#| tbl-pos: H

fish_density_ung |> 
  filter(density_100m2 > 0) |> 
  group_by(reef, family) |> 
  summarise(density_100m2 = median(density_100m2)) |> 
  ungroup() |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = reef, values_from = density_100m2) |> 
  arrange(desc(`Chama Kangu`)) |> 
  flextable::flextable() |> 
  flextable::set_header_labels(family = "Family") |> 
  # flextable::add_header_row(values = c("", "Fish Density (individuals per 100m2)"), colwidths = c(1, 4)) |> 
  flextable::colformat_double(digits = 1, na_str = "-") |> 
  flextable::align(j = 2:5, align = "center", part = "all") |> 
  flextable::bold(part = "header") |> 
  # flextable::bg(part = "header", bg = "#004763", i = 2) |> 
  # flextable::color(part = "header", color = "white", i = 2) |> 
  flextable::fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") |> 
  flextable::bg(j = 2:5, bg = scales::col_quantile(palette = "Reds", domain = c(0,95), na.color = "#cfceceff"), part = "body") |> 
  flextable::theme_vanilla() |>
  flextable::width(j = 1, width = 1.5, unit = "in") |>
  flextable::width(j = 2:5, width = .8, unit = "in") |> 
  flextable::fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") |>
  flextable::font(fontname = "Alegreya", part = "all")  |> 
  flextable::padding(padding = 2.2)

```


```{r}
#| label: fig-fish-density-unguja-error
#| fig-cap: "Median fish density (individuals per 100 m^2^) across the surveyed reefs in Unguja North."
#| fig-pos: H

fish_density_ung |> 
  mutate(reef = as.factor(reef)) |> 
  # filter(density_100m2 > 0 ) |> 
  tidyplot(x = reef, y = density_100m2) |> 
  add_sem_errorbar(width = .15, linewidth = .5) |> 
  # add_ci95_errorbar(width = .15, linewidth = .5) |> 
  add_mean_bar() |>
  # add_data_points_beeswarm() |> 
  adjust_size(width = 4.5, height = 3, unit = 'in') |> 
 adjust_font(fontsize = 10) |> 
 adjust_y_axis(title = 'Fish Density (individuals per 100m^2^')|>  
  reorder_x_axis_labels(c('Chama Kangu','Mwamba Mrefu',  'Mayale', 'Chumani') |> rev()) |> 
  remove_x_axis_title() |> 
  remove_legend_title()

```



### Coral Recruits {#sec-unguja-recruits}

\index{coral recruits!Unguja North}

Coral recruitment in Unguja North was dominated by *Galaxea* and *Porites* across most sites, with *Chama Kangu* exhibiting the highest overall recruit densities, particularly for *Galaxea* (250 ind. 100 m^-2^) and *Fungia* (200 ind. 100 m^-2^). *Chumani* and *Mayale* showed strong recruitment of *Acropora* and *Fangia* (200 ind. 100 m^-2^ respectively), while *Mwamba Mrefu* recorded the highest density of *Porites* recruits (200 ind. 100 m^-2^). The presence of pioneer genera like *Pocillopora* and *Millepora* at *Chumani* suggests active recovery potential, whereas the absence of *Acropora* recruits at *Mwamba Mrefu* may indicate localized settlement limitations for branching coral species (see @fig-recruit-density-unguja-error).


```{r}
recruit_ung = readxl::read_excel('data/TNC Data_Unguja/Unguja coral survey_March 17th -20th 2026.xlsx', sheet = 'Coral Recruits') |> 
  janitor::clean_names()

# fish_ung |> glimpse()

## since the fish biomass column does not have the reef, we remap  using the benthic substrate
recruit_ung = recruit_ung |> 
  left_join(
be_ung |> 
  distinct(location,site) |> 
  rename(reef = site)
  ) |> 
  relocate(reef, .after = site)
```

```{r}
recruit_ung = recruit_ung |> 
  select(site, reef,family=genus, starts_with("x")) |> 
  # names()
  mutate(
    area_m2 = 1*1, 
    x0_2_5cm = as.integer(x0_2_5cm),
    x2_6_5cm = as.integer(x2_6_5cm),
    x6_10cm = as.integer(x6_10cm),
    ) |> 
  rowwise() |> 
  mutate(total_count = sum(c_across(starts_with("x")), na.rm = TRUE)) |> 
  mutate(density_100m2 = (total_count / area_m2) * 100)


```


```{r}
#| label: tbl-recruit-density-unguja
#| tbl-cap: "Recruit density (individuals per 100 m^2^) by family across the surveyed reefs in Unguja North."
#| tbl-pos: H

recruit_ung |> 
  filter(density_100m2 > 0) |> 
  group_by(reef, family) |> 
  summarise(density_100m2 = median(density_100m2)) |> 
  ungroup() |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = reef, values_from = density_100m2) |> 
  arrange(desc(`Chama Kangu`)) |> 
  flextable::flextable() |> 
  flextable::set_header_labels(family = "Family") |> 
  flextable::add_header_row(values = c("", "Recruit Density (individuals per 100m2)"), colwidths = c(1, 4)) |> 
  flextable::colformat_double(digits = 0, na_str = "-")|> 
  flextable::bold(part = "header") |> 
  # flextable::bg(part = "header", bg = "#004763", i = 2) |> 
  # flextable::color(part = "header", color = "white", i = 2) |> 
  flextable::fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") |> 
  flextable::bg(j = 2:5, bg = scales::col_quantile(palette = "Reds", domain = c(100,350), na.color = "#cfceceff"), part = "body")  |> 
  flextable::theme_vanilla() |>
  flextable::width(j = 1, width = 1.5, unit = "in") |>
  flextable::width(j = 2:5, width = .8, unit = "in") |> 
  flextable::fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") |>
  flextable::font(fontname = "Alegreya", part = "all")  |> 
  flextable::padding(padding = 2.2)  |> 
  flextable::align(j = 2:5, align = "center", part = "all") 
```


```{r}
#| label: fig-recruit-density-unguja-error
#| fig-cap: "Median recruit density (individuals per 100 m^2^) across the surveyed reefs in Unguja North."
#| fig-pos: H

recruit_ung |> 
  mutate(reef = as.factor(reef)) |> 
  tidyplot(x = reef, y = density_100m2) |> 
  add_mean_bar(alpha = 0.4) |>
  add_sem_errorbar(width = .2, linewidth = .8) |> 
  add_data_points_beeswarm(size = 1.2, alpha = 0.6) |> 
  adjust_size(width = 5, height = 3.5, unit = 'in') |> 
 adjust_font(fontsize = 10) |> 
 adjust_y_axis(title = expression(Recruit~Density~(ind.~100*m^-2)))|>  
  reorder_x_axis_labels(c('Mwamba Mrefu',  'Mayale', 'Chumani', 'Chama Kangu') ) |> 
  remove_x_axis_title() |> 
  remove_legend_title()

```



### Invertebrate Assemblages {#sec-unguja-inverts}

\index{invertebrates!Unguja North}

Invertebrate assemblages in Unguja North were heavily dominated by sea urchins (@tbl-invertebrate-unguja), particularly at **Mayale**, which recorded a density of 282 individuals per 100 m^2^, significantly higher than **Mwamba Mrefu** (16 ind. 100 m^-2^) and **Chumani** (9 ind. 100 m^-2^). Other taxa were present in much lower abundances; blue starfish and white starfish were only recorded at **Chumani** (3 and 1 ind. 100 m^-2^ respectively), while sea cucumbers were only observed at **Chama Kangu** (2 ind. 100 m^-2^). These low densities of high-value invertebrates like sea cucumbers across all sites reflect the persistent pressure of historical harvesting.


```{r}
invert_ung = readxl::read_excel('data/TNC Data_Unguja/Unguja coral survey_March 17th -20th 2026.xlsx', sheet = 'Invertebrates_TIDY') |> 
  janitor::clean_names()

# fish_ung |> glimpse()

## since the fish biomass column does not have the reef, we remap  using the benthic substrate
invert_ung = invert_ung |> 
  left_join(
    be_ung |> 
      distinct(location,site) |> 
      rename(reef = site)
  ) |> 
  relocate(reef, .after = site)



## Functional groups
invert_ung_restoration = invert_ung  |> 
  rename(genus = common_name) |> 
  mutate(restoration_role = case_when(
    genus == "Sea Urchin" ~ "Algal Grazer",
    genus == "sea cucumber " ~ "Nutrient Cycler",
    genus == "Lobster" ~ "Predator",
    genus == "COTs" ~ "Coral Predator (Threat)",
    TRUE ~ "General Invertebrate"
  ))


 invert_ung_restoration = invert_ung_restoration |> 
  select(location, reef, genus, total_count = counts, restoration_role) |> 
  # pivot_longer(cols = Urchin:COTs, names_to = 'genus', values_to = 'total_count') |> 
   mutate(area_m2 = 50*2, .before = genus) |> 
   mutate(total_count = na_if(total_count, 0)) |> 
  mutate(density_100m2 = (total_count / area_m2) * 100) 

```



```{r}
#| eval: true
#| label: tbl-invertebrate-unguja
#| tbl-cap: "Mean recruit density (individuals per 100 m^2^) by family across the surveyed reefs in Unguja. Values represent the median density observed across all transects per site."
#| tbl-pos: H

invert_ung_restoration |> 
  select(location, reef, family = genus, density_100m2) |> 
  # mutate(density_100m2 = na_if(density_100m2, 0)) |> 
filter(density_100m2 > 0) |> 
  group_by(reef, family) |> 
  summarise(
    density_mean = median(density_100m2, na.rm = TRUE)
    # density_sd = sd(density_100m2, na.rm = TRUE)
    ) |> 
  ungroup() |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = reef, values_from = density_mean) |> 
  arrange(desc(`Chumani`)) |> 
  flextable::flextable() |> 
  flextable::set_header_labels(family = "Family") |> 
  # flextable::add_header_row(values = c("", "Invertebrates (individuals per 100m2)"), colwidths = c(1, 4)) |> 
  flextable::colformat_double(digits = 0, na_str = "-") |> 
  flextable::bold(part = "header") |> 
  # flextable::bg(part = "header", bg = "#004763", i = 2) |> 
  # flextable::color(part = "header", color = "white", i = 2) |> 
  flextable::fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") |> 
  flextable::bg(j = 2:5, bg = scales::col_quantile(palette = "Reds", domain = c(0,20), na.color = "#cfceceff"), part = "body")  |> 
  flextable::theme_vanilla() |>
  flextable::width(j = 1, width = 1.5, unit = "in") |>
  flextable::width(j = 2:5, width = .8, unit = "in") |> 
  flextable::fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") |>
  flextable::font(fontname = "Alegreya", part = "all")  |> 
  flextable::padding(padding = 2.2)|> 
  flextable::align(j = 2:5, align = "center", part = "all") 

```



```{r}
#| label: fig-inverts-mtwara-site1
#| fig-cap: "Indicator invertebrate density (individuals per 100 m^2^) by taxon across reefs in Mnazi Bay and Msangamkuu."
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| fig-pos: H
#| eval: false



plot_obj = invert_ung_restoration |> 
  mutate(reef = str_replace_all(reef, 'Chamba Cha Kati', 'Kati')) |> 
  tidyplot(y = reef, x = density_100m2, color = restoration_role) |> 
  add_sum_bar(preserve = 'total') |> 
  adjust_colors(new_colors = c(
        "Nutrient Cycler"   = "#4393C3",
        "Algal Grazer" = "#099b55ff",
        "Coral Predator (Threat)" = "#ff0505ff",
        "Predator"   = "#F4A582",
        "General Invertebrate"   = "#0d91b5ff"
      )) |> 
  add_data_points_beeswarm(size = 1, alpha = 0.5) |> 
  adjust_font(fontsize = 12) |>
  adjust_size(width = 1.2, height = 1.5, unit = 'in')  |> 
  adjust_legend_title(title = 'Restoration Role', fontsize = 13, face = 'bold') |> 
  adjust_legend_position(position = c(.15,1.20))|>
  adjust_y_axis_title(title = 'Survey Site') |> 
  adjust_x_axis_title(title = 'Density (ind./100m2)')|> 
  remove_padding() |> 
#   remove_x_axis_title() |> 
  split_plot(by = genus, ncol = 3) 

plot_obj |> ggsave(filename = "invert_density_unguja.pdf")


```


![Invertebrate density (individuals per 100 m^2^) by taxon across reefs in Unguja North.](invert_density_unguja2.pdf){#fig-inverts-mtwara fig-pos="H" width="100%"}



## Mtwara {#sec-mtwara}

### Benthic Substrate Composition {#sec-mtwara-substrate}

The benthic community structure in Mtwara reflects a diverse coral reef ecosystem, with substrate composition varying significantly across the surveyed sites. At the site level, hard coral dominance was most pronounced at Chamba Cha Kati (80.8%), Matenga (80.6%), and Kieti (79.4%), as shown in @tbl-substrate-mtwara. In contrast, Mmaluko exhibited a more heterogeneous composition with high coralline algae (43.9%) and sand (26.3%). Fleshy algae were most prevalent at Viulu (26.9%) and Mmaluko (23.8%), while seagrass was notably present only at Rasmo (22.5%) and Mmaluko (11.5%).

```{r}
#| label: tbl-substrate-mtwara
#| tbl-cap: Benthic substrate composition across survey locations in Mtwara
#| tbl-pos: H

be_mt |>
 select(location, substrate_category, pct_cover) |>
 filter(pct_cover > 0) |> 
 group_by(location, substrate_category) |>
 summarise(pct_cover = median(pct_cover)) |>
 ungroup() |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = location, values_from = pct_cover)  |> 
  select(Substrate = substrate_category , `Chamba Cha Kati`, Membelwa =`Inner Membelwa`, Matenga, Kieti, Rasmo, Viulu, Nusura, Mmaluko) |>
  flextable::flextable() |>
  flextable::set_header_labels(substrate_category = "Substrate") |>
#   flextable::add_header_row(values = c("", "Percentage Cover"), colwidths = c(1, 8)) |>
#   flextable::bold(part = "header") |>
#   flextable::bg(part = "header", bg = "#004763", i = 2) |>
#   flextable::color(part = "header", color = "white", i = 2)|>
  flextable::colformat_double(digits = 1, na_str = "-") |>
  flextable::align(j = 2:4, align = "center", part = "all") |>
  flextable::bg(j = 2:9, bg = scales::col_quantile(palette = "Reds", domain = c(0,100), na.color = "#cfceceff"), part = "body") |>
  flextable::width(j = 1:3, width = .90, unit = "in")  |>
  flextable::width(j = 4:9, width = .55, unit = "in") |> 
  theme_vanilla()  |>
  flextable::fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") |>
  flextable::font(fontname = "Alegreya", part = "all") |> 
  flextable::padding(padding = 2.5, part = "all") |>
  flextable::align(j = 2:9, align = "center", part = "all")

```


Statistical analysis of the benthic substrate reveals significant differences in reef composition based on management status (@tbl-mtwara-substrate-test). As illustrated in @fig-mtwara-substrate, sites located within Marine Management Areas (MMAs) exhibit significantly higher hard coral cover compared to outside areas ($p < 0.001$), where algae and rubble dominate the landscape. Conversely, sites outside MMAs are characterized by significantly higher levels of algae ($p = 0.001$) and rubble/other substrates ($p = 0.036$). No significant differences were observed for seagrass or soft coral cover between the two management regimes.


```{r}
#| label: fig-mtwara-substrate
#| fig-cap: "Benthic substrate composition across survey locations in Mtwara region, categorized by management status (Inside vs. Outside MMA)."
#| fig-pos: H

be_mt |> 
    mutate(major_category = fct_reorder(major_category, pct_cover, .fun = mean)) |> 
    tidyplot(y = major_category, x = pct_cover, color = management) |>
    add_mean_bar(preserve = 'total') |> 
    add_sem_errorbar() |>
  # add_test_pvalue(method = "wilcoxon")|> 
    # add_test_asterisks()|> 
    # adjust_colors(new_colors =  c("Hard Coral" = "#D73027", "Soft Coral" = "#762A83", "Algae" = "#4575B4", "Seagrass" = "#1B7837", "Rubble/Others" = "#E0E0E0"
    # )) |> 
    adjust_size(width = 3, height = 2, unit = "in") |> 
    adjust_font(fontsize = 10)  |> 
    adjust_legend_title(title = "Management") |> 
    adjust_legend_position(position = c(.65,.3)) |>
    adjust_x_axis_title(title = "Percent Cover") |> 
    remove_y_axis_title()
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-mtwara-substrate-test
#| tbl-cap: "Results of t-tests comparing percent cover of major benthic substrate categories between sites inside vs. outside marine management areas (MMAs) in Mtwara region."
#| tbl-pos: H
#| 
be_mt |> 
  group_by(major_category) |> 
  rstatix::t_test(pct_cover ~ management) |> 
  rstatix::add_significance() |> 
  select(major_category, `Within` = n1, `Outside` = n2, statistic, p, p.signif) |> 
  flextable() |> add_header_row(values = c("", "Sample Size (N)", "Sample Size (N)", "", "", ""), top = TRUE)|> 
  merge_at(i = 1, j = 2:3, part = "header") |> 
  set_header_labels(major_category = "Category", statistic = "t-stat", p = "p-value", p.signif = "Sig.") |> 
  colformat_double(j = "p", digits = 3) |> 
  colformat_double(j = "statistic", digits = 2) |> 
  width(j = 1:6, width = .7, unit = "in") |> 
  fontsize(size = 11 , part = "all") |>
  flextable::font(fontname = "Alegreya", part = "all") |>
  theme_vanilla() |> 
  flextable::padding(padding = 2.5, part = "all") |>
  align(j = 2:6, align = "center", part = "all")

```


### Fish Biomass {#sec-mtwara-fish}

\index{fish biomass!Mtwara}

Fish assemblages across Mtwara reefs showed pronounced spatial variability in density and family composition (@fig-fish-biomass-mtwara-error). **Chamba Cha Kati** (Mnazi Bay) recorded the highest fish densities overall, with Pomacentridae dominating at 254.6 individuals per 100 m^2^ — nearly ten times the densities observed at **Nusura** (26.0) and **Viulu** (22.0) (@tbl-fish-biomass-mtwara). Herbivorous families, including Scaridae and Acanthuridae, were present across all surveyed sites, with Scaridae density peaking at Chamba Cha Kati (11.0 ind. 100 m^-2^). **Viulu** stood out for comparatively higher densities of Siganidae (4.4) and Mullidae (5.0). Families such as Holocentridae and Balistidae were recorded exclusively at Chamba Cha Kati, while the unprotected reefs at Nusura and Viulu maintained higher densities of Lethrinidae and Pomacanthidae. These patterns suggest that although the protected reef at Chamba Cha Kati has greater overall fish biomass and damselfish abundance, the adjacent unprotected reefs continue to support functionally diverse assemblages that are important for broader reef resilience.



```{r}
#| eval: true
#| label: tbl-fish-biomass-mtwara
#| tbl-cap: "Mean fish density (individuals per 100 m^2^) by family across the surveyed reefs in Mtwara. Values represent the median density observed across all transects per site."
#| tbl-pos: H

fish_density |> 
  select(site, reef, family, density_100m2) |> 
  # mutate(density_100m2 = na_if(density_100m2, 0)) |> 
filter(density_100m2 > 0) |> 
  group_by(reef, family) |> 
  summarise(
    density_mean = median(density_100m2, na.rm = TRUE)
    # density_sd = sd(density_100m2, na.rm = TRUE)
    ) |> 
  ungroup() |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = reef, values_from = density_mean) |> 
  arrange(desc(`Chamba Cha Kati`)) |> 
  flextable::flextable() |> 
  flextable::set_header_labels(family = "Family") |> 
  flextable::add_header_row(values = c("", "Fish Density (individuals per 100m2)"), colwidths = c(1, 3)) |> 
  flextable::colformat_double(digits = 1, na_str = "-") |> 
  flextable::align(j = 2:4, align = "center", part = "all") |> 
  flextable::bold(part = "header") |> 
  flextable::bg(part = "header", bg = "#004763", i = 2) |> 
  flextable::color(part = "header", color = "white", i = 2) |> 
  flextable::fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") |> 
  flextable::bg(j = 2:4, bg = scales::col_quantile(palette = "Reds", domain = c(0,300), na.color = "#cfceceff"), part = "body")  |>
  flextable::width(j = 1:3, width = 1.20, unit = "in")  |>
  # flextable::width(j = 4:9, width = .55, unit = "in") |> 
  theme_vanilla()  |>
  flextable::fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") |>
  flextable::font(fontname = "Alegreya", part = "all") |> 
  flextable::padding(padding = 2.5, part = "all") |>
  flextable::align(j = 2:4, align = "center", part = "all")
```




```{r}
#| label: fig-fish-biomass-mtwara-error
#| fig-cap: "Median fish density (individuals per 100 m^2^ +/- SE) across surveyed reefs in Mtwara. The dashed blue line represents the regional depletion threshold of 20 individuals per 100 m^2^, indicating that most surveyed reefs currently sustain fish populations above critical depletion levels."
#| tbl-pos: H
#| 

fish_density |> 
  tidyplot(x = reef, y = density_100m2, fill = site) |> 
  add_sem_errorbar(width = .2) |> 
  add_mean_bar(alpha = 1) |> 
  adjust_font(fontsize = 10) |>
  adjust_legend_position(position = c(.6,.4)) |> 
  adjust_size(width = 2.8, height = 3, unit = 'in') |> 
  adjust_y_axis(title = 'Fish Density (individuals per 100m^2^', breaks = seq(3,20,3)) |> 
  remove_x_axis_title() |> 
  remove_legend_title()

```


```{r}
#| eval: false

fish_density |> 
  select(site, reef, family, density_100m2) |> 
  # mutate(density_100m2 = na_if(density_100m2, 0)) |> 
filter(density_100m2 > 0) |> 
  group_by(site, reef) |> 
  summarise(
    density_mean = mean(density_100m2, na.rm = TRUE),
    density_sd = sd(density_100m2, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n())
    ) |> 
  ungroup() |>
    ggplot(aes(x = reef, y = density_mean, fill = site))+
    geom_errorbar(
      aes(ymin = density_mean - density_sd, ymax = density_mean + density_sd),
      width = 0.1, linewidth = 0.6
    ) +
    geom_col(width = 0.65, colour = "white")  +
    geom_hline(
      yintercept = 20, linetype = "dotted",
      colour = "darkblue", linewidth = 0.4
    ) +
    annotate(
      "text", x = Inf, y = 21.5,
      label = "Depletion threshold (20 individual/100m2)",
      hjust = 1.25, size = 3.2, colour = "darkblue"
    ) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c('firebrick', 'steelblue') |> rev(), guide = "none") +
    scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Fish Density (ind.~100m2", 
    breaks = scales::pretty_breaks(n = 5),
    expand = c(0, 0),
    limits = c(0, 60)
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(), axis.title.x = element_blank()) 


```

### Coral Recruits {#sec-mtwara-recruits}

\index{coral recruits!Mtwara}

Coral recruitment across Mtwara reefs revealed considerable taxonomic diversity and spatial heterogeneity among the eight surveyed sites (@fig-recruit-mtwara-error). At **Chamba Cha Kati**, recruitment was dominated by framework-building genera, with *Acropora* and *Merulina* each reaching densities of 500 ind. 100 m^-2^, and *Alveopora*, *Lobophyllia*, and *Millepora* contributing equally at 300 ind. 100 m^-2^. **Nusura** stood out for its exceptionally high *Porites* recruit density (650 ind. 100 m^-2^), a pattern typical of reefs recovering from disturbance, where massive corals tend to dominate early successional stages. At **Viulu** and **Kieti**, recruitment was broadly distributed across *Alveopora*, *Favia*, and *Stylophora* (400--450 ind. 100 m^-2^), suggesting relatively stable and diverse recruitment assemblages. The occurrence of specialist genera — *Gardineroseris*, *Halomitra*, and *Herpolitha* — exclusively at Chamba Cha Kati, alongside consistently elevated *Acropora* densities at **Kieti** (400 ind. 100 m^-2^), points to meaningful recovery potential across the study region (@tbl-recruit-mtwara).



```{r}
#| eval: true
#| label: tbl-recruit-mtwara
#| tbl-cap: "Recruit density (individuals per 100 m^2^) by family across the surveyed reefs in Mtwara. Values represent the median density observed across all transects per site."
#| tbl-pos: H

recruit_density |> 
  select(site, reef, family = genus, density_100m2) |> 
  filter(density_100m2 > 0) |> 
  group_by(reef, family) |> 
  summarise(
    density_mean = median(density_100m2, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = reef, values_from = density_mean)  |> 
  select(family, `Chamba Cha Kati`, Membelwa = `Inner Membelwa`, Matenga, Kieti, Rasmo, Viulu, Nusura, Mmaluko) |> 
  arrange(desc(`Chamba Cha Kati`)) |> 
  flextable::flextable() |> 
  flextable::set_header_labels(family = "Family") |> 
  flextable::add_header_row(values = c("", "Recruit Density (individuals per 100m2)"), colwidths = c(1, 8)) |> 
  flextable::colformat_double(digits = 0, na_str = "-") |> 
  flextable::theme_vanilla()|> 
  flextable::align(j = 2:4, align = "center", part = "all") |> 
  flextable::bold(part = "header") |> 
  # flextable::bg(part = "header", bg = "#004763", i = 2) |> 
  # flextable::color(part = "header", color = "white", i = 2) |> 
  flextable::bg(j = 2:9, bg = scales::col_quantile(palette = "Reds", domain = c(10,700), na.color = "#cfceceff"), part = "body") |> 
  # flextable::autofit() |> 
  flextable::width(j = 1:3, width = .80, unit = "in")  |> 
  flextable::width(j = 4:9, width = .60, unit = "in")    |>
  flextable::fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") |>
  flextable::font(fontname = "Alegreya", part = "all") |> 
  flextable::padding(padding = 2.5, part = "all") |>
  flextable::align(j = 2:9, align = "center", part = "all")

```




```{r}
#| label: fig-recruit-mtwara-error
#| fig-cap: "Median recruit density (individuals per 100 m^2^ +/- SE) across surveyed reefs in Mtwara."
#| tbl-pos: H
#| 

recruit_density |> 
  select(site, reef, family = genus, density_100m2) |> 
  tidyplot(y = reef, x = density_100m2) |> 
  add_sem_errorbar(width = .2) |> 
  add_mean_bar(alpha = 1) |> 
  adjust_font(fontsize = 10) |>
  adjust_size(width = 2.8, height = 2.5, unit = 'in') |> 
  adjust_x_axis(title = 'Recruit Density (individuals per 100m^2^', breaks = seq(50,400,50)) |> 
  remove_y_axis_title()

```

### Invertebrate Assemblages {#sec-mtwara-inverts}

\index{invertebrates!Mtwara}

```{r}
invert_density_restoration = invert_density  |> 
  mutate(restoration_role = case_when(
    genus == "Urchin" ~ "Algal Grazer",
    genus == "Sea Cucumber" ~ "Nutrient Cycler",
    genus == "Lobster" ~ "Predator",
    genus == "COTs" ~ "Coral Predator (Threat)",
    TRUE ~ "General Invertebrate"
  ))
```



Indicator invertebrate densities in Mtwara varied significantly across the eight surveyed reefs (@tbl-invertebrate-mtwara1). **Viulu** exhibited the highest overall densities for several key groups, including sea stars (900 ind. 100 m^-2^), urchins (1,150 ind. 100 m^-2^), and gastropods (800 ind. 100 m^-2^). High densities were also observed at **Rasmo** (650 sea stars) and **Kieti** (600 gastropods). **Nusura**, **Rasmo**, and **Mmaluko** supported the highest recorded densities of sea cucumbers (300 ind. 100 m^-2^). In contrast, **Chamba Cha Kati** and **Membelwa** generally recorded lower invertebrate abundances, though Chamba Cha Kati maintained stable populations of bivalves (300 ind. 100 m^-2^). Notably, Crown-of-Thorns starfish (COTs), a significant coral predator, were recorded at **Viulu** (200 ind. 100 m^-2^) and **Kieti** (100 ind. 100 m^-2^, *See* @fig-inverts-mtwara), representing localized threats to reef recovery.


```{r}
#| eval: true
#| label: tbl-invertebrate-mtwara1
#| tbl-cap: "Invertebrate density (individuals per 100 m^2^) by family across the surveyed reefs in Mtwara."
#| tbl-pos: H

invert_density_restoration |> 
  select(Site, reef, family = genus, density_100m2) |> 
  # mutate(density_100m2 = na_if(density_100m2, 0)) |> 
filter(density_100m2 > 0) |> 
  group_by(reef, family) |> 
  summarise(
    density_mean = median(density_100m2, na.rm = TRUE)
    # density_sd = sd(density_100m2, na.rm = TRUE)
    ) |>
  ungroup() |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = reef, values_from = density_mean) |> 
  # relocate(Membelwa, .after = 2) |> 
  select(family, `Chamba Cha Kati`, Membelwa, Matenga, Kieti=kieti, Rasmo, Viulu, Nusura, Mmaluko) |>
  arrange(desc(`Chamba Cha Kati`)) |> 
  flextable::flextable() |> 
  flextable::set_header_labels(family = "Family") |> 
  flextable::colformat_double(digits = 0, na_str = "-") |> 
  flextable::align(j = 2:4, align = "center", part = "all") |> 
  flextable::bold(part = "header") |> 
  # flextable::bg(part = "header", bg = "#004763", i = 2) |> 
  # flextable::color(part = "header", color = "white", i = 2) |> 
  flextable::fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") |> 
  flextable::bg(j = 2:9, bg = scales::col_quantile(palette = "Reds", domain = c(100,1500), na.color = "#cfceceff"), part = "body") |> 
  flextable::width(j = 1:3, width = .90, unit = "in")  |> 
  flextable::width(j = 4:9, width = .60, unit = "in")  |> 
  theme_vanilla() |>
  flextable::fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") |>
  flextable::font(fontname = "Alegreya", part = "all") |> 
  flextable::align(j = 2:9, align = "center", part = "all") |> 
  flextable::padding(padding = 2.5, part = "all")

```



```{r}
#| label: fig-inverts-mtwara-site
#| fig-cap: "Indicator invertebrate density (individuals per 100 m^2^) by taxon across reefs in Mnazi Bay and Msangamkuu."
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| fig-pos: H
#| eval: false

# ggplot(data = invert_density_restoration, aes(x = reef, y = density_100m2, fill = restoration_role)) +
#   geom_col(position = "dodge")  +
#     scale_fill_manual(
#       values = c(
#         "Nutrient Cycler"   = "#4393C3",
#         "Algal Grazer" = "#099b55ff",
#         "Coral Predator (Threat)" = "#ff0505ff",
#         "Predator"   = "#F4A582",
#         "General Invertebrate"   = "#0d91b5ff"
#       )
#     ) +
#   facet_wrap(~genus, scales = "free_y") +
#   theme_minimal() +
#   theme(
#     legend.position = "top",
#     legend.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
#     axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 1),
#     panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
#   )+
#   labs(
#     # title = "Invertebrate Density and Ecological Roles in Mtwara",
#     # subtitle = "Higher grazer density (Urchins) can assist in algal control for restoration",
#     y = "Density (ind. per 100m2)",
#     x = "Survey Reefs",
#     fill = "Restoration Role"
#   )


plot_obj = invert_density_restoration |> 
  mutate(reef = str_replace_all(reef, 'Chamba Cha Kati', 'Kati')) |> 
  tidyplot(y = reef, x = density_100m2, color = restoration_role) |> 
  add_sum_bar() |> 
  adjust_colors(new_colors = c(
        "Nutrient Cycler"   = "#4393C3",
        "Algal Grazer" = "#099b55ff",
        "Coral Predator (Threat)" = "#ff0505ff",
        "Predator"   = "#F4A582",
        "General Invertebrate"   = "#0d91b5ff"
      )) |> 
  add_data_points_beeswarm(size = 1, alpha = 0.5) |> 
  adjust_font(fontsize = 12) |>
  adjust_size(width = 1.5, height = 1.5, unit = 'in')  |> 
  adjust_legend_title(title = 'Restoration Role', fontsize = 13, face = 'bold') |> 
  adjust_legend_position(position = c(.80,.15))|>
  adjust_x_axis_title(title = 'Density (ind./100m2)')|> 
  remove_padding() |> 
  remove_y_axis_title() |> 
  split_plot(by = genus, ncol = 3) 

# ggsave(filename = "invert_density_mtwara_all.pdf", plot = plot_obj)


```

![Invertebrate density (individuals per 100 m^2^) by taxon across reefs in Mnazi Bay and Msangamkuu.](invert_density_mtwara_all.pdf){#fig-inverts-mtwara fig-pos="H" width="100%"}





# Synthesis and Restoration Priorities {#sec-synthesis}

\index{restoration!priorities}

## Ecological Condition Assessment {#sec-condition}

The conceptual restoration prioritisation framework shown in @fig-conceptual integrates ecological condition and governance performance into a single decision tool for site-level planning. By plotting each reef against these two dimensions, the framework clarifies not only where ecological decline is most severe, but also where institutional capacity is strong enough to support recovery actions. This dual-lens approach improves prioritisation by distinguishing ecological need from management readiness, helping practitioners allocate limited resources more strategically across Unguja North and Mtwara.

The framework classifies sites into four management tiers with clear operational implications. Tier I (Maintain and Monitor) includes high-performing reefs where current governance is functioning and the priority is to preserve gains through compliance and long-term monitoring. Tier II (Urgent Restoration and MMA Establishment) captures sites with poor ecological condition and weak governance, requiring immediate intervention alongside formal management strengthening. Tier III (Active Restoration and Governance) represents ecologically degraded reefs that already have some governance structures in place, where restoration can be accelerated through improved enforcement and co-management. Tier IV (Formalise Governance) includes comparatively healthy but unprotected reefs, where proactive legal and institutional designation is needed to prevent future decline and lock in existing resilience.

As illustrated in @fig-conceptual, sites falling in the lower-left quadrant --- **Chama Kangu** and **Mwamba Mrefu** in Unguja North, and **Mmaluko** in Mtwara --- represent the highest restoration priority. Despite high recruit densities at Chama Kangu, extreme algal dominance ($>75\%$) and very low coral cover indicate a persistent phase shift that requires active intervention. In Mtwara, **Chamba Cha Kati** remains the regional benchmark for reef health, and together with **Inner Membelwa**, **Matenga**, and **Kieti**, forms a high-condition cluster where maintenance and compliance should be prioritized. **Nusura** and **Viulu** show moderate-to-high ecological resilience but weaker governance, while **Rasmo** sits near the management threshold; these sites are strong candidates for formal MMA establishment and strengthened co-management to safeguard existing coral framework and recovery potential.


```{r}
#| label: fig-conceptual
#| fig-cap: "Conceptual restoration prioritisation framework. Sites are plotted by composite ecological condition score (y-axis; derived from benthic cover, fish biomass, recruit density, and invertebrate assemblages) against governance/MMA effectiveness score (x-axis). Dashed lines represent regional mean thresholds. Quadrant labels indicate recommended management actions."
#| fig-height: 5.5

condition_df <- tibble(
  site      = c("Chumani", "Mayale", "Chama Kangu", "Mwamba Mrefu", 
                "Chamba Cha Kati", "Nusura", "Viulu", "Inner Membelwa", 
                "Matenga", "Kieti", "Rasmo", "Mmaluko"),
  region    = c(rep("Unguja North", 4), rep("Mtwara", 8)),
  gov_score = c(75, 70, 40, 45, 95, 55, 50, 90, 85, 80, 45, 40),
  eco_score = c(65, 58, 35, 30, 92, 68, 60, 88, 85, 82, 55, 45),
  mma       = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, 
                TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE)
)

ggplot(
  condition_df,
  aes(x = gov_score, y = eco_score,
      colour = region, shape = mma, label = site)
) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 60, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 55, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +
  geom_point(size = 5) +
  ggrepel::geom_label_repel(
    size = 3, box.padding = 0.45,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = region_colours) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values = c(`TRUE` = 19, `FALSE` = 21),
    labels = c("MMA site", "Non-MMA site")
  ) +
  annotate("text", x = 43, y = 93,
           label = "III. Active restoration + governance",
           size = 3, colour = "grey35", fontface = "italic") +
  annotate("text", x = 78, y = 93,
           label = "I. Maintain + monitoring",
           size = 3, colour = "grey35", fontface = "italic") +
  annotate("text", x = 43, y = 17,
           label = "II. Urgent restoration + MMA establishment",
           size = 3, colour = "grey35", fontface = "italic") +
  annotate("text", x = 78, y = 17,
           label = "IV. Formalise governance",
           size = 3, colour = "grey35", fontface = "italic") +
  labs(
    x      = "Governance / MMA Effectiveness Score (%)",
    y      = "Ecological Condition Score (%)",
    colour = "Region",
    shape  = "MMA Status"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(30, 100), ylim = c(10, 100)) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```


## MMA Effectiveness {#sec-mmaeffect}

\index{marine managed areas (MMAs)!effectiveness}

The assessment confirms that formal management is strongly associated with better reef condition in Mtwara. Protected reefs—**Chamba Cha Kati**, **Inner Membelwa**, **Matenga**, and **Kieti** (inside MBREMP)—generally maintain higher hard coral cover and more favorable coral-to-algae ratios than open-access reefs. Outside formal management, **Nusura** and **Viulu** retain moderate ecological value but show weaker governance signals, while **Rasmo** and **Mmaluko** indicate greater vulnerability through lower overall condition and higher non-coral substrate dominance. Notably, Crown-of-Thorns starfish (COTs) were recorded at **Viulu** and **Kieti**, highlighting ongoing biological pressure that requires targeted surveillance and rapid-response control, including at sites with otherwise strong recovery potential.

## Restoration Recommendations {#sec-recommendations}

\index{restoration!recommendations}

Based on the assessment findings, a tiered restoration framework is recommended to address the varying levels of degradation and management needs across the regions (see @tbl-restoration). Immediate priority is assigned to the most critically degraded sites in Unguja North, specifically Chama Kangu and Mwamba Mrefu. Chama Kangu exhibits extreme algal dominance (76%) and minimal coral cover (5%), necessitating active macroalgae removal and coral outplanting alongside formal MMA establishment. Similarly, Mwamba Mrefu suffers from high macroalgae and low fish density, requiring the deployment of artificial reef structures and community-led herbivore protection. In Mtwara, moderate-priority sites like Viulu and Kieti face biological threats from Crown-of-Thorns starfish (COTs) and lack formal protection; these areas require COTs removal programs and inclusion into formal management frameworks to prevent further decline.

Sites categorized for maintenance represent high-quality habitats that serve as ecological anchors for the region. Chamba Cha Kati, Matenga, and Inner Membelwa in Mtwara all maintain exceptional hard coral cover (~80%), requiring strict enforcement and routine monitoring to safeguard their status as regional donor sites for coral fragments. In Unguja North, Mayale and Chumani show moderate resilience but face specific pressures such as high sea urchin-driven bioerosion (282 ind/100m²) and low invertebrate diversity. Recommendations for these sites focus on maintaining MCA patrols, invertebrate restocking—particularly sea cucumbers—and strengthening local co-management to reduce localized stressors. This tiered approach ensures that resources are strategically allocated between urgent active recovery and the long-term preservation of existing marine assets.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-restoration
#| tbl-cap: "Tiered restoration recommendations by site, prioritised by ecological condition and MMA governance status in Unguja North and Mtwara."
#| tbl-pos: H

# Fix: use the real assignment operator `<-`
restoration_table <- tibble(
  Priority = c("Immediate", "Immediate", "Moderate", "Moderate", "Moderate", "Moderate",
               "Maintenance", "Maintenance", "Maintenance", "Maintenance", "Maintenance", "Maintenance"),
  Site = c("Chama Kangu", "Mwamba Mrefu", "Viulu", "Nusura", "Kieti", "Rasmo",
           "Mayale", "Chumani", "Chamba Cha Kati", "Inner Membelwa", "Matenga", "Mmaluko"),
  Region = c("Unguja North", "Unguja North", "Mtwara", "Mtwara", "Mtwara", "Mtwara",
             "Unguja North", "Unguja North", "Mtwara", "Mtwara", "Mtwara", "Mtwara"),
  `Key Issues` = c("Extreme algal dominance (76%); low coral cover (5%)", "High macroalgae; low coral cover (8.8%); low fish density",
                   "Presence of COTs; high algal cover (26.9%); no formal protection", "High coral cover but lacks formal management; sea urchin dominance",
                   "Moderate algal pressure; COTs observed; no formal protection", "High sea star density; elevated sea cucumber densities; outside MMA",
                   "High sand cover; high urchin density (282 ind/100m2)", "Moderate coral cover; low invertebrate diversity",
                   "Excellent condition (80% coral); high fish biomass", "High ecological condition but requires sustained enforcement",
                   "High hard coral cover (~80%); maintain recovery trajectory", "Mixed benthic condition; elevated fleshy/coralline algae and seagrass"),
  `Recommended Actions` = c("Active macroalgae removal; coral outplanting; MMA establishment",
                            "Artificial reef structures; herbivore protection; community management",
                            "COTs removal program; formal MMA inclusion; monitoring",
                            "Establishment of community-led no-take zone; urchin management",
                            "Targeted COTs surveillance/removal; herbivore protection; MMA inclusion",
                            "Strengthen local compliance; protect key herbivores; targeted monitoring",
                            "Monitoring of urchin-driven bioerosion; maintain MCA patrols",
                            "Invertebrate restocking (sea cucumbers); maintain MCA patrols",
                            "Strict protection; use as regional donor site for coral fragments",
                            "Maintain strict enforcement; long-term coral and fish monitoring",
                            "Routine ecological monitoring; protect herbivores; maintain compliance",
                            "Reduce local stressors; targeted coral outplanting; strengthen co-management")
)

# restoration_table |>
#   flextable::as_grouped_data(groups = "Priority") |> 
#   flextable::flextable() |> 
#   flextable::theme_vanilla() |> 
#   flextable::bold(j = 1, i = ~ !is.na(Priority), bold = TRUE, part = "body") |> 
#   flextable::bold(part = "header") |> 
#   flextable::bg(part = "header", bg = "#004763") |> 
#   flextable::color(part = "header", color = "white") |> 
#   flextable::fontsize(size = 10, part = "all") |> 
#   flextable::width(j = 4:5, width = 2) |> 
#   flextable::width(j = 1:3, width = .5)


restoration_table |> 
  flextable() |> 
  merge_v(j = c(1,3)) |> 
  theme_vanilla()  |> 
  flextable::bold(j = 1, i = ~ !is.na(Priority), bold = TRUE, part = "body") |> 
  # flextable::bold(part = "header") |> 
  # flextable::bg(part = "header", bg = "#004763") |> 
  # flextable::color(part = "header", color = "white")|> 
  flextable::width(j = 4:5, width = 2) |> 
  flextable::width(j = 1:3, width = .7) |> 
  flextable::padding(padding = 2.5, part = "all") |> 
  flextable::align(j = 1:5, align = "left", part = "all") |> 
  flextable::font(fontname = "Alegreya", part = "all")  |> 
  flextable::fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") 
```


## Conclusions {#sec-conclusions}

This assessment provides an integrated, multi-metric baseline of reef condition across Unguja North and Mtwara under the *Bahari Yetu* project. Key findings are:

1. **Reference reefs:** **Chamba Cha Kati** remains the strongest benchmark site, with consistently high hard coral cover (about 80%), high fish density, and broad recruit diversity. In Mtwara, **Matenga**, **Kieti**, and **Inner Membelwa** also show strong ecological condition and function as key maintenance sites.

2. **Critical degradation hotspots:** **Chama Kangu** and **Mwamba Mrefu** (Unguja North) remain the highest-priority restoration sites, with very low hard coral cover and strong algal dominance, indicating persistent phase-shifted states requiring active intervention.

3. **Management effects are evident:** Sites inside MMAs show significantly higher hard coral cover and lower algal/rubble dominance than outside sites, reinforcing the ecological value of formal management. At the same time, non-MMA reefs such as **Nusura** and **Viulu** still retain important ecological value and should be prioritized for strengthened governance.

4. **Recruitment and recovery potential:** Coral recruitment is substantial but spatially uneven. **Chamba Cha Kati** and **Kieti** show strong framework-builder recruitment (including *Acropora*), while **Nusura** is dominated by high *Porites* recruitment, indicating differing recovery trajectories among sites.

5. **Emerging invertebrate risks and restoration pathway:** Invertebrate assemblages show marked site variability, with localized Crown-of-Thorns occurrence (notably **Viulu** and **Kieti**) and continued pressure on high-value taxa. A tiered strategy is supported: immediate active restoration in degraded Unguja reefs, targeted threat control and governance expansion in moderate-priority Mtwara reefs, and strict protection/monitoring of high-condition benchmark reefs.

\newpage

# References {#sec-references .unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
